summary: Spolsky makes two observations: first, a humorous jab at the pointless 'conventions used in this document' sections found in every technical manual. Second, he discusses incremental installation tools for software updates, noting that competition from Red Bend Software's vBuild has driven prices down from RTPatch's original $5000, making binary patching more viable for small software companies like Fog Creek.
key_points: Technical writing conventions sections are self-evident wastes of space that exist seemingly to pad page counts
Competition in the incremental installer market (Red Bend vs RTPatch) drove prices down significantly, from $5000 to around $2500-2750
Even advocates of 'best tools money can buy' must weigh tool cost against actual savings — RTPatch at $5000 was disproportionate to bandwidth costs
Spolsky is willing to switch from a free, excellent tool (InnoSetup) to a paid, merely decent one (InstallShield) to gain access to incremental patching
money_quotes: Why does every technical manual and book include a section at the beginning on 'conventions used in this document,' full of ridiculous and useless tips like, 'Tips are indicated by a lightbulb icon in the margin'?
The familiar dot (.) symbol from Internet addresses shall be used on this website to denote the end of a sentence.
Their salesman called me up to give me a hard time for contradicting myself. After all, step 9 in the Joel Test is using 'the best tools that money can buy.' 'Touché,' I said, and hung up.
Still pricey but starting to seem worth it.
Today I'm going to try converting CityDesk's setup from InnoSetup, which is excellent and free, to InstallShield, which is decent and expensive, just to take advantage of vBuild.
key_insight: Competition drives software tool prices down, and pragmatic developers must constantly reassess the cost-benefit tradeoffs of their tooling choices as the market evolves.
